title: One Library to Rule Them All: Why We Built OneRingAI
author: Anton Antich
text:
One Library to Rule Them All: Why We Built OneRingAI
Part 0 of the Building with OneRingAI series — the origin story, the architecture, and the desktop app.
We didn’t set out to build another AI framework.
We were building Everworker — an enterprise platform for AI-powered automation — and needed an agent library that could talk to any LLM vendor, manage tools and authentication uniformly, handle context windows intelligently, and do it all in TypeScript without pulling in a sprawling dependency graph.
We looked at what existed: LangChain, CrewAI, the usual suspects. They didn’t fit. LangChain was 50+ packages of abstraction layers that made simple things hard and debugging harder. CrewAI was Python-only and opinionated about agent roles in ways that didn’t match our use cases. Everything felt like it was designed for demos, not production.
So we built OneRingAI. Then we open-sourced it. Then we built a desktop app on top of it to make it easy for you to try.
This post introduces the library, its architecture, and HOSEA — the free desktop app that puts it all in your hands. It’s Part 0 of a series where we’ll go deep into each piece.
See it in action in this short video or read on!
What OneRingAI Is
OneRingAI is a TypeScript/Node.js library for building AI agents. One library. One import. Twelve native vendors. A hundred built-in tools. Smart context management. And the kind of authentication system you’d actually want to use in production.
npm i @everworker/oneringai
import { Connector, Agent, Vendor } from '@everworker/oneringai';
// Register your API key once
Connector.create({
name: 'openai',
vendor: Vendor.OpenAI,
auth: { type: 'api_key', apiKey: process.env.OPENAI_API_KEY! },
});
// Create an agent
const agent = Agent.create({
connector: 'openai',
model: 'gpt-4',
tools: [readFile, webSearch, bash],
});
// Run it
const response = await agent.run('Analyze the latest sales data and write a summary.');
console.log(response.output);
That’s it. No chains, no graphs, no prompt templates, no vector store abstractions. An agent with tools, connected to a provider, doing work.
Want to switch to Anthropic? Change two lines:
Connector.create({
name: 'claude',
vendor: Vendor.Anthropic,
auth: { type: 'api_key', apiKey: process.env.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY! },
});
const agent = Agent.create({ connector: 'claude', model: 'claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929' });
Everything else — tools, memory, context management, session persistence — works identically across vendors. That’s the point.
The same setup in Hosea is of course even easier:
Why Not LangChain? Why Not CrewAI?
This isn’t about bashing other frameworks. They are great for certain uses. But if you’re starting a serious project in 2025/2026, the landscape looks different.
LangChain pioneered the space, but it grew into an abstraction maze: 50+ packages, 4+ layers of indirection, and a Python-first ecosystem that treats TypeScript as a second-class citizen. Simple things require deep framework knowledge. Debugging means stepping through layers of chains, callbacks, and output parsers. Authentication is scattered across integrations with no unified pattern.
CrewAI is elegant if you think in terms of agent crews and roles, but it’s Python-only, opinionated about agent orchestration, and doesn’t natively handle the things production apps need — multimedia generation, desktop automation, context window management, or multi-tenant authentication.
We wrote a detailed comparison at oneringai.io if you want the full breakdown. But the short version:
Where OneRingAI differs:
- One library, not fifty. OneRingAI is a single package with 20 dependencies. LangChain is 50+ packages with layers of abstraction between you and the actual API call. CrewAI is growing a similar ecosystem. We believe in a cohesive library, not a framework of frameworks.
- TypeScript-first. Strict mode, full type safety, ESM. LangChain treats JS/TS as a second-class citizen. CrewAI is Python-only. If your stack is Node.js, OneRingAI is built for you.
- Auth that actually works. A unified connector registry with named credentials, OAuth 2.0 flows, and AES-256-GCM encryption. LangChain scatters authentication across per-integration code. We centralize it because auth is a production concern, not an afterthought.
- Smart context management. A plugin architecture with compaction strategies and real-time token budgeting. Not a sliding window. Not “just use a vector store.” Actual, pluggable algorithms that decide what to keep, what to compress, and what to move to external storage.
- Native multimedia. Image generation, video generation, text-to-speech, speech-to-text — built in, not bolted on. Same connector system, same tool interface.
- Desktop and Browser automation. 11 built-in tools for screenshot, mouse, keyboard, and window control, about 20 for controlling the browser. No other agent framework ships this.
- Battle-tested. ~3,000 unit tests, tens of production Enterprise customers. This isn’t a weekend project — it’s the core of a production enterprise platform.
OneRingAI is what you’d build if you started fresh today, knowing everything that went wrong with the first generation of AI frameworks.
The Architecture in 5 Minutes
Four pillars hold up the library. Each one is designed to be understood in an afternoon and extended in an hour.
1. Connectors — Authentication as a First-Class Primitive
Every external service — whether it’s an LLM vendor, a REST API, or an OAuth-protected SaaS product — is accessed through a Connector. Connectors are a central registry for authentication, resilience, and configuration — and, they enable standard TOOLS (all of them — “universal API call”, ever growing number — customized to specific systems!)
// LLM vendor
Connector.create({
name: 'openai',
vendor: Vendor.OpenAI,
auth: { type: 'api_key', apiKey: '...' },
});
// SaaS integration with OAuth
Connector.create({
name: 'github',
serviceType: Services.Github,
baseURL: 'https://api.github.com',
auth: { type: 'oauth2', clientId: '...', clientSecret: '...' },
});
// Multiple keys for the same vendor
Connector.create({ name: 'openai-fast', vendor: Vendor.OpenAI, auth: { ... } });
Connector.create({ name: 'openai-cheap', vendor: Vendor.OpenAI, auth: { ... } });
Named connectors mean you can have multiple credentials per vendor (production vs. staging, fast vs. cheap), built-in retry and circuit breaker policies, and a single place to manage all authentication. No more scattered API keys in environment variables, no more per-integration auth code.
This is of course visual in Hosea:
2. Plugins — Composable Context Management
This is the heart of what makes OneRingAI different, and it’s what (upcoming) Part 1 of this series dives deep into.
An AI agent’s context window is a shared, finite resource. System prompt, conversation history, memory, tools — they all compete for the same space. Most frameworks treat these as separate, special-cased systems. We treat them as plugins: self-contained modules that contribute content, track their own token usage, provide tools, and know how to shrink when space gets tight.
Built-in plugins:
- Working Memory — External storage with a compact index in context. The agent stores megabytes of data; the LLM sees only descriptions and retrieves on demand. Three-tier system (raw/summary/findings) for research workflows.
- In-Context Memory — Key-value store that appears directly in the system message. For state that needs to be visible every turn: progress counters, current status, session preferences.
- Persistent Instructions — Keyed entries saved to disk that survive across sessions. The agent’s accumulated knowledge: “Always use TypeScript,” “The user’s company is Acme Corp.”
All three are optional, toggled via feature flags, and you can build your own plugin in ~60 lines of code. There is more packaged and more are added constantly. Seeing and fully controlling your Agent’s insides is priceless:
3. Strategies — Intelligent Compaction
When context gets full, something has to give. Most frameworks use a crude sliding window — oldest messages get dropped. OneRingAI uses compaction strategies: pluggable algorithms that decide what to trim, what to move to Working Memory, and what to protect.
The default algorithmic
strategy:
- Moves large tool results to Working Memory (preserving access via retrieval)
- Limits the number of tool pairs kept in context
- Applies a rolling window to conversation history
- Asks compactable plugins to evict low-priority entries
Strategies are pluggable — you can register your own. We’ll cover this in depth in Part 4 of the series.
4. Tools — 100+ Built-In, Unified Execution
Tools in OneRingAI are just objects with a definition and an execute
function. No special wrappers, no framework-specific abstractions:
const myTool: ToolFunction = {
definition: {
type: 'function',
function: {
name: 'get_weather',
description: 'Get current weather for a city.',
parameters: {
type: 'object',
properties: { city: { type: 'string' } },
required: ['city'],
},
},
},
execute: async (args) => {
const data = await fetchWeather(args.city);
return { temperature: data.temp, conditions: data.conditions };
},
};
The library ships with 100+ tools across categories: filesystem (read, write, edit, glob, grep), shell (bash execution), web (search via Serper/Brave/Tavily, scrape via ZenRows/Jina/Firecrawl), desktop automation (screenshot, mouse, keyboard, window management), code execution, document reading (PDF, DOCX, XLSX), and more.
Every tool goes through ToolManager
— unified execution with per-tool circuit breakers, permission levels, namespacing, and lifecycle hooks.
HOSEA: The Desktop App That Proves It All Works
A library is only as good as the product built on it. Ours is HOSEA — Human-Oriented System for Engaging Agents.
HOSEA is a free, cross-platform desktop app (macOS, Windows, Linux) that puts the full power of OneRingAI behind a polished GUI. It’s not a demo. It’s the app we use every day. Installation is as easy as downloading packaged installer or you can run a development version from the oneringai repo.
What HOSEA Does
Multi-vendor AI chat. Connect to OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Groq, Mistral, DeepSeek, Ollama, and more — all in one interface. Switch models mid-conversation. Compare responses across vendors.
Agent builder. Create specialized agents with custom system prompts, curated tool sets, and specific memory configurations. A research agent with Working Memory and web search tools. A coding agent with filesystem tools and persistent instructions. A customer support agent connected to Zendesk and Salesforce via universal connectors.
Multimedia studio. Generate images (DALL-E 3, Google Imagen, Grok), create videos (Sora, Veo), convert text to speech — all from the same app, using the same connector system.
40+ service integrations templates, unlimited integrations with AI-first generation. Connect to GitHub, Slack, Stripe, Salesforce, Notion, HubSpot, Jira, Shopify, and more. One-click OAuth flows. Each connected service automatically becomes a tool your agents can use.
Local AI with Ollama. Run models locally without API keys. HOSEA manages the Ollama installation, downloads models based on your system RAM, and auto-starts the server.
The “Look Inside” panel. This is the feature builders love. A sidebar that shows you exactly what the agent sees: the full system message, plugin contents, token budget breakdown, Working Memory index. No black box. Full transparency.
Dynamic UI. Agents can create live dashboards, progress displays, and rich visualizations in the sidebar — charts (Vega-Lite), diagrams (Mermaid), mind maps (Markmap), formatted tables, and more. The agent writes to In-Context Memory with showInUI: true
, and HOSEA renders it with the same Markdown engine as the chat window.
Free Forever
HOSEA is free. No subscription, no usage limits, no premium tier. Bring your own API keys (BYOK) and you’re set. The app is built on the open-source OneRingAI library, and the binary is distributed via GitHub Releases.
If you want centralized key management for a team, that’s what the optional Everworker backend is for — but it’s entirely optional. HOSEA works perfectly standalone.
The State of the Project
OneRingAI is in active development with almost daily releases (and yes, of course we use Agents to help code — but we do it in a smart and controlled way). Here’s where things stand:
- Core library (
@everworker/oneringai
): v0.4.2, 62K+ LOC, 2,345+ unit tests, 12 vendors, 100+ tools - HOSEA desktop app: v0.1.8, available for macOS/Windows/Linux
- Documentation: oneringai.io
- License: Open source for the library, commercial but free to use for Hosea
What we’re building next: more compaction strategies (including LLM-powered summarization), agent-to-agent delegation, expanded MCP ecosystem support, and a plugin marketplace for HOSEA.
What’s Coming in This Series
This is Part 0 — the big picture. The rest of the series goes deep:
- Part 1: Plugin Architecture for Context Management — How we designed a composable plugin system for agent memory, and how to build your own plugin in 60 lines. (Published)
- Part 2: Connectors and Multi-Vendor Abstraction — The connector-first architecture, named credentials, OAuth flows, and why authentication deserves to be a first-class primitive.
- Part 3: Tool Management and Permissions — Unified tool execution, per-tool circuit breakers, ConnectorTools for auto-discovered API integrations, and the permission system.
- Part 4: Compaction Strategies — How to keep an agent running for hundreds of turns without losing its mind. Algorithmic compaction, plugin-aware eviction, and building custom strategies.
- Part 5: Building a Full Agent — From
Agent.create()
to a production-ready assistant with memory, tools, persistence, and session management.
Get Started
Install the library:
npm i @everworker/oneringai
Download HOSEA:
Read the installation instructions.
Read the docs:
oneringai.io has the full API reference, getting started guide, and comparison with other frameworks.
Star the repo:
If this resonates, give us a star on GitHub. It helps more than you’d think.
We built OneRingAI because we needed it. We open-sourced it because we think the AI agent ecosystem deserves better primitives. And we built HOSEA because the best way to prove a library works is to ship a real product on it.
If you’re building AI agents — or thinking about it — we’d love for you to try it. And if you find something broken, tell us. That’s how this gets better.
Next up: Part 1 — Plugin Architecture for Context Management. Follow for the rest of the series.
